In particular, this means that \(x' \in X'\) is the point realizing the minimal distance to \(x\). Again by Proposition 7.45, \(x'-x\) is therefore orthogonal to \(W'\). Switching the role of \(X\) and \(X'\) we obtain similarly that \(x-x'\) is orthogonal to \(W\).

4. \(\Rightarrow\) 1.: Our assumption means that

\[ x - x' \in W^\bot \cap W'^\bot = (W + W')^\bot = Z^\bot. \]

To see the latter equality note that some vector is orthogonal to \(W + W'\) precisely if it is orthogonal to \(W\) and to \(W'\), by the bilinearity of \({\left \langle -, - \right \rangle}\). We use this remark as follows: from

\[ x - x' = v+w - v' - w' \]

we get

\[ \begin{align*} d = v-v' & = \underbrace{x-x'}_{\in Z^\bot} + \underbrace{w' - w}_{\in Z}. \end{align*} \]

By the unicity of the representation of \(d\) as a sum of a vector in \(Z^\bot\) and one in \(Z\), this means that \(x-x' = p_{Z^\bot}(d) = m\). ◻

Example 7.60

We consider the two lines in \({\bf R}^3\)

\[ \begin{align*} X & = (2,-1,3) + L(1,1,-2) = v + W\\ X' & = (-3,0,0) + L(0,2,4) = v' + W'. \end{align*} \]

The general vectors of \(X\) and \(X'\) are of the following form, for \(a, b \in {\bf R}\).

\[ \begin{align*} x &= (2,-1,3) + a(1,1,-2) & = (2+a,-1+a,3-2a) \\ x'& = (-3,0,0) + b(0,2,4) & = (-3,2b,4b) \\ x-x' & & = (5+a,-1+a-2b,3-2a-4b) \end{align*} \]

We compute the minimal distance of \(X\) and \(X'\) by considering the condition \(x - x' \bot (1,1,-2)\) and \(x - x' \bot (0,2,4)\). This gives the following homogeneous linear system

\[ \begin{align*} 0 & = (5+a)+(-1+a-2b)-2(3-2a-4b) & = -2+6a+6b \\ 0 & = 2(-1+a-2b)+4(3-2a-4b) & = 10-6a -20 b.\\ \end{align*} \]

This can be solved to \(b = \frac 47\) and \(a = - \frac 5 {21}\). The points \(x\) and \(x'\) and their distance is then readily computed.

docs.title: Summary of some computational methods
docs.text:
Task 7.61

Compute an orthonormal basis of a subspace \(U \subset {\bf R}^n\).

Method: First compute any (not necessarily orthonormal) basis of \(U\). Then orthonormalize that basis using the Gram–Schmidt algorithm (Proposition 7.32).

Task 7.62

Given \(x \in {\bf R}^n\) and a subspace \(U \subset {\bf R}^n\), compute the orthogonal projection \(p_U(x)\).

Method: Compute an orthonormal basis \(u_1, \dots, u_k\) of \(U\) (cf. Task 7.61), then use

\[ p_U(x) = \sum_{i=1}^k {\left \langle x, u_i \right \rangle} u_i. \]

One may also compute \(U^\bot\) (cf. Task 7.63), then compute \(p_{U^\bot}(x)\), and finally \(p_U(x) = x-p_{U^\bot}(x)\). This may be easier in practice, especially if \(\dim U > \dim U^\bot\).

Task 7.63

Compute the orthogonal complement \(U^\bot\) of a given a subspace \(U \subset {\bf R}^n\).

Method: Compute a basis \(u_1, \dots, u_k\) of \(U\). Then solve the homogeneous system

\[ {\left \langle x, u_1 \right \rangle} = 0, \dots, {\left \langle x, u_k \right \rangle} = 0. \]

Equivalently, form the matrix

\[ \left ( \begin{array}{c} u_1 \\ \dots \\ u_k \end{array} \right ) \]

(whose rows are the basis vectors \(u_1\) etc.), and compute its kernel.

The next two tasks are related to the different representations of lines.

Task 7.64

Convert a line \(L\) from cartesian equations (as in Definition 7.53) to a vector/parametric equation (as in Definition 7.50).

Method: Pick two distinct points \(p, q\) satisfying the cartesian equations. Then write

\[ L = p + L(p-q). \]

Choosing different points instead of \(p, q\) will give different vector equations; but if \(L = p' + L(p'-q')\), the two vectors \(p-q\) and \(p'-q'\) will necessarily be (non-zero) multiples of each other (by Lemma 7.41).

Task 7.65

Convert a line from vector equation form (Definition 7.50) \(L = v+L(w)\) into cartesian equations.

Method: Express one coordinate in terms of the free parameter and substitute into the other coordinate equations.

Task 7.66

Compute a cartesian equation for the plane \(P\) containing three non-collinear points \(p, q, r \in {\bf R}^3\).

Method: First write

\[ P = p + L(q-p, r-p). \]

Then compute a normal vector to \(L(q-p, r-p)\) and write the corresponding cartesian equation. As in Task 7.64, different choices of \(p, q, r\) may give different intermediate vectors, but they span the same underlying subspace.

Task 7.67

Given a line \(L\) and a point \(r \notin L\), find the plane \(P\) containing \(L\) and \(r\).

Method: Choose two distinct points \(p, q \in L\), then apply Task 7.66 to the three points \(p, q, r\).

Task 7.68

Given a plane \(P = \{x \mid {\left \langle x, a \right \rangle} = d \}\), a line \(L = v + L(w) \subset P\), and a point \(p \in P\), find the line \(M \subset P\) through \(p\) orthogonal to \(L\).

Method: Write \(M = p + L(w')\). Determine \(w'\) by imposing \(w' \bot a\) and \(w' \bot w\), i.e., compute

\[ w' \in (L(a,w))^\bot \]

as in Task 7.63.

Task 7.69

Given a plane in vector form \(P = v + L(w_1, w_2)\), compute a cartesian equation

\[ P = \{x \in {\bf R}^3 \mid {\left \langle x, a \right \rangle} = d \}. \]

Method: Compute \(W=L(w_1,w_2)\) and then \(W^\bot\). Choose any non-zero \(a \in W^\bot\), and set

\[ d={\left \langle a, v \right \rangle}. \]

Note: a presentation of \(P\) as above is not unique, but another presentation as \(P = \{x \in {\bf R}^3 | {\left \langle x, a' \right \rangle} = d'\}\) will be such that \(a = \lambda a'\) for some \(\lambda \in {\bf R}, \lambda \ne 0\), and \(d = \lambda d'\).

Task 7.70

Decide whether two lines are parallel.

Method: If the lines are in parametric form (\(L = v+L(w)\) and \(L' = v'+L(w')\)), check whether \(w\) and \(w'\) are linearly dependent. If the lines are given in cartesian form, first convert them via Task 7.64.

Task 7.71

Decide whether two lines \(L = v+L(w)\) and \(L' = v'+L(w')\) are skew.

Method: Verify both conditions: (a) \(w\) and \(w'\) are linearly independent, and (b) \(L \cap L' = \emptyset\). For condition (b), it is often convenient to use cartesian equations first (cf. Task 7.65).

Task 7.72

Decide whether a line \(L = v+L(w)\) is parallel to a plane \(P\).

Method: If \(P = v' + L(w'_1,w'_2)\), check whether \(w\) is a linear combination of \(w'_1,w'_2\) (equivalently, \(W \subset W'\)). If \(P = \{x \mid {\left \langle x, a \right \rangle}=d\}\), check whether \({\left \langle w,a \right \rangle}=0\).

Task 7.73

Decide whether a line \(L = v+L(w)\) is orthogonal to a plane \(P\).

Method: If \(P = \{x \mid {\left \langle x, a \right \rangle}=d\}\), check whether \(w\) and \(a\) are linearly dependent. If \(P = v'+L(w'_1,w'_2)\), check whether \({\left \langle w,w'_1 \right \rangle}=0\) and \({\left \langle w,w'_2 \right \rangle}=0\).

Task 7.74

Compute the distance of two affine subspaces \(X = v + W\) and \(X' = v' + W'\) (including the point case \(W=\{0\}\)).

Method: Use one of the following equivalent approaches:

  • apply Theorem 7.59 via part 1.: compute \(Z = W+W'\), \(d := v-v'\), and compute the orthogonal projection \(m = p_{Z^\bot}(d)\), or equivalently \(m = d-p_Z(d)\) (cf. Task 7.62). Let \(x\) be a general point in \(X\), \(x'\) a general point in \(X'\), and solve the linear system \(x-x'=m\), – or –,

  • apply Theorem 7.59 via part 4.: again let \(x\) be a general point in \(X\), \(x'\) a general point in \(X'\), compute \(x-x'\) and solve the homogeneous linear system given by \({\left \langle x, w \right \rangle} = 0\) and \({\left \langle x, w' \right \rangle} = 0\), where \(w\) runs through a set of vectors spanning \(W\), and \(w'\) runs through a set of vectors spanning \(W'\).

docs.title: Exercises
docs.text:
Exercise 7.1

(See Solution 7.9.1.) Let \(V = P_{\le 2} = \{ at^2 + bt+c \ | \ a,b,c \in {\bf R} \}\) be the vector space of (real) polynomials of degree \(\le 2\). We consider the scalar product in Example 7.163., i.e.,

\[ {\left \langle p, q \right \rangle} = \int_{-1}^1 p(x)q(x) dx. \]

  • Let \(e_1 = 1\), \(e_2 = t\) and \(e_3 = t^2\). (These vectors form a basis of \(P_{\le 2}\).) Compute \({\left \langle e_i, e_j \right \rangle}\) for \(1 \le i,j \le 3\). (This requires knowledge of basic integration techniques.)

  • Apply the Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization procedure to this basis.

Exercise 7.2

(See Solution 7.9.3.) Consider the subspace \(U \subset {\bf R}^3\) given by the solutions of the homogeneous linear system

\[ x-y+3z=0. \]

  1. Find a basis of \(U\).

  2. Compute a basis of \(U^\bot\). What is \(\dim U^\bot\)?

  3. Consider \(t=(0,1,5)\). Find its orthogonal projection onto \(U\) (recall from Corollary 7.34 that \(t = t_U + t_\bot\) with uniquely determined vectors \(t_U \in U\) and \(t_\bot \in U^\bot\). The orthogonal projection of \(t\) onto \(U\) is then the vector \(t_U\).)

Exercise 7.3

(See Solution 7.9.2.) Consider the subspace \(W \subset {\bf R}^4\) given by the equations

\[ \begin{align*} x-t & = 0\\ y+z-t & = 0\\ \end{align*} \]

(where \(x,y,z,t\) are the coordinates of \({\bf R}^4\)).

  1. Compute a basis of \(W\) and of \(W^\bot\).

  2. Compute the orthogonal projection of \(t=(1,5,1,6)\) onto \(W\).

Exercise 7.4

(See Solution 7.9.4.) Consider the subspace \(U \subset {\bf R}^3\) given by the equations

\[ \begin{align*} x & = 0\\ x+y+z & = 0\\ \end{align*} \]

(where \(x,y,z\) are the coordinates of \({\bf R}^3\)).

  1. Compute a basis of \(U\) and of \(U^\bot\).

  2. Compute the orthogonal projection of \(t=(5,1,3)\) onto \(U\).

Exercise 7.5

(See Solution 7.9.5.) Compute the orthogonal complement of \(T = L((1,0,-3))\).

Exercise 7.6

(See Solution 7.9.6.) Is there a subspace \(U \subset {\bf R}^3\) such that

  1. the orthogonal projection of \(t=(1,1,0)\) onto \(U\) is given by \((1,5,6)\)?

  2. the orthogonal projection of \(t=(2,0,1)\) onto \(U\) is given by \((1,1,1)\)?

Exercise 7.7

(See Solution 7.9.7.) Let \(L = \left ( \begin{array}{c} 1 \\ 3 \\ 5 \end{array} \right ) + L (\left ( \begin{array}{c} 1 \\ 1 \\ 4 \end{array} \right ))\). Compute the closest point of \(L\) to the origin, and its distance to the origin.

Exercise 7.8

(See Solution 7.9.8.) Consider the two lines \(L: x=1+t,y=t,z=2+t, t \in {\bf R}\) and \(L':x-3=y-1=z-3\). Are they parallel? Compute the distance between \(L\) and \(L'\).

Exercise 7.9

(See Solution 7.9.9.) Are the lines

\[ L: x = y-1=-z \text{ and } L' :x-2=-y=\frac z2 \]

identical, parallel, or skew? Compute their distance.

Exercise 7.10

(See Solution 7.9.10.) Let \(P\) be the plane given by the equation

\[ 4x+5y+10z-20 = 0. \]

Let \(L\) be the line given by the equations \(x = 0, y = 5 - z\).

  1. Sketch \(P\) and \(L\).

  2. Compute the orthogonal complement of the underlying vector space \(W\) of \(P\).

  3. Compute the point of \(P\) that is closest to the origin and its distance to the origin.

  4. Are \(P\) and \(L\) parallel?

Exercise 7.11

(See Solution 7.9.11.) Which of the following matrices is orthogonally diagonalizable? If so, find a orthonormal eigenbasis of \({\bf R}^2\).

  1. \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array} \right )\)

  2. \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -2 & 1 \end{array} \right )\)

  3. \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array} \right )\).

Exercise 7.12

(See Solution 7.9.12.) Let \(A\) be a symmetric matrix and \(\lambda \ne \mu\) two distinct eigenvalues of \(A\), with eigenvectors \(v\) and \(w\), respectively. Then \(v \bot w\), i.e., eigenvectors of distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.

Exercise 7.13

(See Solution 7.9.13.) Let \(P \subset {\bf R}^4\) by the hyperplane given by

\[ 2 x_1 + x_3 - x_4 = 4, \]

where \((x_1, \dots, x_4)\) are the coordinates of \({\bf R}^4\). For a parameter \(t \in {\bf R}\), let \(L_t\) be the line

\[ L_t = (1,0,0,-2t) + L(t, 1, 0, -1). \]

  • For which \(t \in {\bf R}\) is \(L_t\) parallel to \(P\)?

  • Let now \(t = -\frac 12\) and consider the line \(L = L_{-\frac 12}\). Determine the pair(s) of points \((p, l)\) such that \(p \in P\) and \(l \in L\) such that their distance is minimal.

Exercise 7.14

(See Solution 7.9.14.) Let \(L \subset {\bf R}^3\) be the line defined be the system

\[ \begin{align*} x+z & = 1\\ y+z & = -2. \end{align*} \]

Let \(L'\) be the line in \({\bf R}^3\) passing through the points \((0,0,1)\) and \((0,1,1)\).

  • Present \(L\) as \(L=v+W\) for a subspace \(W \subset {\bf R}^3\). Do the same for \(L'\).

  • Are \(L\) and \(L'\) a) identical, b) parallel, c) skew or d) intersecting?

  • Compute the Cartseian equation (i.e., in the form \(ax+by+cz=d\), for appropriate values of \(a, \dots, d\)) of the plane \(P \subset {\bf R}^3\) that contains \(L\) and is parallel to \(L'\).

  • Let \(l = (2,-1,-1) \in L\). Compute a point \(l' \in L'\) such that the line passing through \(l\) and \(l'\) is parallel to the plane given by the equation \(x+z-1=0\).

Exercise 7.15

(See Solution 7.9.15.) Let \(V = P_{\le 2}\) be the vector space of polynomials of degree \(\le 2\). We write elements of \(V\) as \(p(t)=a+bt+ct^2\), where \(a,b,c\in{\bf R}\). Define

\[ {\left \langle p, q \right \rangle} := \int_{-1}^1 p(t) q(t) dt. \]

  • Confirm that \({\left \langle -, - \right \rangle}\) is a scalar product on \(V\).

  • Compute an orthonormal basis of \(V\).

  • Consider the map

\[ f : V \to V, f(p) := t \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} \]

(i.e., it maps a polynomial \(p\) to the product of the indeterminate \(t\) with the derivative of \(p\) with respect to the variable \(t\)). Confirm that this map is linear. Compute the matrix of \(f\) with respect to the standard basis \(e_1 = 1\), \(e_2 = t\) and \(e_3 = t^2\). Is this basis an eigenbasis for \(f\)? Compute \(\dim \ker f\) and \(\dim {\operatorname{im}\ } f\).

  • Does the map \(f\) have an orthonormal eigenbasis?

Exercise 7.16

(See Solution 7.9.16.) Consider the subspace \(U \subset{\bf R}^4\) given by the solutions of the equation

\[ x_1-x_2+x_3+2x_4 = 0. \]

(As usual \(x_1, \dots, x_4\) are the coordinates of \({\bf R}^4\).)

  1. Find a basis of \(U\). What is \(\dim U\)?

  2. Compute an orthonormal basis of \(U\).

  3. Compute the orthogonal projection of \(v=(2,3,0,0)\) and of \(w=(2,5,3,0)\) onto \(U\).

  4. Compute \(U^\bot\).

Exercise 7.17

(See Solution 7.9.17.) In the Euclidean space \({\bf R}^4\), endowed with the standard scalar product, let \(U\) be the subspace spanned by the vectors \(u_1 = (1,2,0,-1)\), \(u_2 = (0,-4,3,4)\).

  1. Compute an orthogonal basis of \(U\).

  2. Compute a basis of \(U^\perp\).

  3. Compute the orthogonal projection of \(v = (0,5,3,4)\) onto \(U\).

  4. Let \(w = (2,-1,0,2)\). Decide whether there is a subspace \(L \subset {\bf R}^4\) such that the orthogonal projection of \(w\) onto \(L\) is the vector \(\ell = (1,1,2,0)\).

Exercise 7.18

(See Solution 7.9.18.) Consider the following two lines in \({\bf R}^3\), where \(x, y, z\) are the coordinates:

\[ L: \left\{\begin{aligned} & x + y - 1 = 0\\ & 2x - z - 1 = 0 \end{aligned}\right. \qquad M: \left\{\begin{aligned} & x - 2y - 1 = 0\\ & y - z + 2 = 0 \end{aligned}\right. \]

  1. Determine whether \(L\) and \(M\) are the same line, parallel, or skew.

  2. Compute the cartesian equation of the plane that contains the line \(M\) and that is parallel to \(L\). (Recall that a cartesian equation is of the form \(\langle x, a \rangle = d\) for an appropriate vector \(a\) and an appropriate \(d \in {\bf R}\).)

  3. Given the point \(l = (0,1,-1) \in L\) compute a point \(m \in M\) such that the line passing through \(l\) and \(m\) is parallel to the plane defined by the equation \(3x - z = 0\).

  4. Consider the family of planes \(\pi_\alpha: z = \alpha\), for some parameter \(\alpha \in {\bf R}\). Let \(r_\alpha = L \cap \pi_\alpha\) and \(s_\alpha = M \cap \pi_\alpha\). Let \(m_\alpha\) be the midpoint of the segment with endpoints \(r_\alpha\) and \(s_\alpha\). Verify that the points \(m_\alpha\) are all lying on the same line. Moreover, determine the parametric equation of that line.

Exercise 7.19

(See Solution 7.9.19.) Consider the points \(p = (3,1,0)\), \(q = (0,1,3)\) and \(r = (-3,0,-3) \in {\bf R}^3\). Let \(L\) be the line passing through \(p\) and \(q\).

  1. Determine the parametric equation of \(L\), i.e., express \(L\) in the form \(L = v + W\), for an appropriate vector \(v \in {\bf R}^3\) and a subspace \(W \subset {\bf R}^3\).

  2. Verify that \(r\) does not lie on \(L\). Give the plane \(P\) containing \(p, q, r\) both in vector and in cartesian form.

Exercise 7.20

(See Solution 7.9.20.) Consider the line \(L = (3,1,0) + L(1,0,-1)\). Is there a plane containing \(L\) and the line \(M\) given by the systen \(x + z = 2\), \(x - 2 y = 2\) (with \(x, y, z\) being the coordinates of \({\bf R}^3\))?

Exercise 7.21

(See Solution 7.9.21.) Consider the line \(L = (3,1,0) + L(1,0,-1)\). Let \(p = (-1,-1,-1)\). Describe all the points \(q \in {\bf R}^3\) such that the line \(M\) passing through \(p\) and \(q\) intersects \(L\) orthogonally (i.e., intersects it, and does so orthogonally).

Exercise 7.22

(See Solution 7.9.22.) Consider the plane \(P = \{x \in {\bf R}^3, 3x - 4y+z = 2\}\) and the point \(p = (0,1,6) \in P\), as well as the line \(L = (0,0,2) + L(1, 1, 1) \subset P\), compute the line \(M \subset P\) that is orthogonal to \(L\) and contains the point \(p\).

Exercise 7.23

(See Solution 7.9.23.) Consider the lines (in \({\bf R}^3\))

\[ \begin{align*} L_1 : \ & 2x-y=-3 \\ & y+z=-2 \end{align*} \]

and

\[ \begin{align*} L_2 : \ & x=t \\ & y = 2 \\ & z = 4-t, t \in {\bf R}. \end{align*} \]

  1. Determine their relative position (skew, parallel etc.)

  2. Find the plane \(\pi\) parallel to \(L_1\) and containing \(L_2\).

  3. Compute the distance between \(\pi\) and \(L_1\).

  4. Find points \(p_1 \in L_1\), \(p_2 \in L_2\) such that

\[ |\hspace{-0.5mm}| {p_1-p_2} |\hspace{-0.5mm}| = d(p_1, p_2) = d(L_1, L_2), \] 
i.e., two points that realize the minimal distance between $L_1$ and $L_2$.

Exercise 7.24

(See Solution 7.9.24.) We endow \({\bf R}^4\) with its usual scalar product. We consider the subspace \(U \subset {\bf R}^4\) defined by the equations

\[ \left\{\begin{aligned} & x_1 + x_3 = 0 \\ & 2x_1 + x_2 - x_4 = 0 \end{aligned}\right. \]

  1. Compute an orthogonal basis of \(U\).

  2. Given the vector \(w_1 = (1,1,-1,-1)\) find a vector \(w_2\) that is orthogonal to \(w_1\) and such that the vector space \(W := L(w_1, w_2)\) satisfies \(W = U^\perp\).

  3. Write down a system of linear equations in the unknowns \(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\) whose solution set is the subspace \(W = U^\perp\).

  4. Given the vector \(v = (3,1,-1,1)\) find a vector \(u \in U\) such that the vector \(v+u\) has minimal norm.

Exercise 7.25

(See Solution 7.9.25.) Consider the lines (contained in \({\bf R}^3\))

\[ L: \left\{\begin{aligned} & x - 2y + 4 = 0\\ & y + z -3 = 0 \end{aligned}\right. \qquad M: \left\{\begin{aligned} & x+2z-5 = 0\\ & x-2y + 1 = 0 \end{aligned}\right. \]

  1. Verify that \(L\) and \(M\) are parallel and write down the cartesian equation of the plane containing \(L\) and \(M\).

  2. Given the point \(p = (0,2,1) \in L\) find the point \(q \in M\) such that the line passing through \(p\) and \(q\) is orthogonal to \(L\) and to \(M\).

  3. Write down the cartesian equation of the plane \(X\) containing the line \(L\) and passing through the point \(r = (-1,1,0)\).

  4. Write down the parametric equation of the line \(N\) contained in the plane \(X\), passing through \(r = (-1,1,0)\) and orthogonal to the line \(L\).

Exercise 7.26

(See Solution 7.9.26.) Let \(U \subset {\bf R}^4\) be the subspace defined by the equations

\[ U: \left\{\begin{aligned} & x_1 - 2x_2 + x_4 = 0 \\ & 3x_2 + x_3 - 2x_4 = 0 \\ & 3x_1 + 2x_3 + t\,x_4 = 0 \end{aligned}\right. \]

  1. Find the value of \(t\) for which \(U\) has dimension 2. For this value of \(t\), compute a basis of \(U\).

  2. Apply the Gram–Schmidt method to the basis computed in part (a) in order to compute an orthogonal basis of \(U\).

  3. For the value of \(t\) computed in part (a), compute a basis of \(U^\perp\).

  4. Given \(v = (3,2,2,-2) \in {\bf R}^4\) compute the cartesian equation of a subspace \(W\) of dimension \(3\) such that the orthogonal projection of \(v\) onto \(W\) is equal to the vector \(w = (1,2,1,1)\).

Exercise 7.27

(See Solution 7.9.27.) We consider the following points (in \({\bf R}^3\)) \(A = (6,-1,-4)\), \(B = (1,1,-1)\) and the plane \(X: 2x - y - 2z = 3\).

  1. Verify that \(B \in X\). Let \(C\) be the orthogonal projection of \(A\) onto the plane \(X\). Compute \(C\).

  2. Compute the cartesian equation of the plane containing the triangle \(\triangle ABC\).

  3. Compute the parametric equation of the line that a) passes through \(B\), b) is contained in the plane \(X\) and c) is orthogonal to the line passing through \(A\) and \(B\).

  4. Compute the value of the parameter \(t\) such that the line \(M_t: \left\{\begin{aligned} & t\,x - y + 2 = 0 \\ & x + z + 1 = 0 \end{aligned}\right.\) is parallel to the plane \(X\).

docs.title: Solutions to the exercises
docs.text:
Solution 7.9.1

(See Exercise 7.1.) This solution illustrates Task 7.61.

We consider \(V=P_{\le 2}\) with the scalar product

\[ {\left \langle p,q ight angle}=\int_{-1}^1 p(t)q(t)\,dt \]

as in Example 7.163.. For \(e_1=1\), \(e_2=t\), \(e_3=t^2\), we compute the scalar products, i.e., the numbers \({\left \langle e_i,e_j ight angle}\):

\[ egin{align*} {\left \langle e_1,e_1 ight angle} &= \int_{-1}^1 1\,dt = \left[ t ight]_{-1}^1 = 2, \ {\left \langle e_1,e_2 ight angle} &= \int_{-1}^1 t\,dt = \left[ rac{t^2}{2} ight]_{-1}^1 = 0, \ {\left \langle e_1,e_3 ight angle} &= \int_{-1}^1 t^2\,dt = \left[ rac{t^3}{3} ight]_{-1}^1 = rac{2}{3}, \ {\left \langle e_2,e_2 ight angle} &= \int_{-1}^1 t^2\,dt = \left[ rac{t^3}{3} ight]_{-1}^1 = rac{2}{3}, \ {\left \langle e_2,e_3 ight angle} &= \int_{-1}^1 t^3\,dt = \left[ rac{t^4}{4} ight]_{-1}^1 = 0, \ {\left \langle e_3,e_3 ight angle} &= \int_{-1}^1 t^4\,dt = \left[ rac{t^5}{5} ight]_{-1}^1 = rac{2}{5}. \end{align*} \]

By symmetry (cf. Lemma 7.5), this also gives the remaining entries with \(i>j\). We now apply Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization (Proposition 7.32):

\[ egin{align*} w_1 &= rac{e_1}{|\hspace{-0.5mm}|{e_1}|\hspace{-0.5mm}|} = rac{1}{\sqrt 2}, \ w_2' &= e_2-{\left \langle e_2,w_1 ight angle}w_1 = t, \qquad w_2= rac{w_2'}{|\hspace{-0.5mm}|{w_2'}|\hspace{-0.5mm}|}=\sqrt{ rac 32}\,t, \ w_3' &= e_3-{\left \langle e_3,w_1 ight angle}w_1-{\left \langle e_3,w_2 ight angle}w_2 = t^2- rac13. \end{align*} \]

Moreover,

\[ |\hspace{-0.5mm}|{w_3'}|\hspace{-0.5mm}|^2 =\int_{-1}^1\left(t^2- rac13 ight)^2dt = rac{8}{45}, \]

so

\[ w_3= rac{w_3'}{|\hspace{-0.5mm}|{w_3'}|\hspace{-0.5mm}|} = rac{\sqrt{10}}{4}(3t^2-1). \]

Therefore one orthonormal basis is

\[ \left( rac{1}{\sqrt 2},\ \sqrt{ rac 32}\,t,\ rac{\sqrt{10}}{4}(3t^2-1) ight). \]

Such polynomials, i.e., orthonormal with respect to the given scalar product, are called Legendre polynomials.

Solution 7.9.2

(See Exercise 7.3.) This solution illustrates Task 7.63, Task 7.62.

Let

\[ W=\{(x,y,z,t)\in { f R}^4\mid x-t=0,\ y+z-t=0\}. \]

From the equations we get \(x=t\) and \(y=t-z\). With free parameters \(z=a\) and \(t=b\), every vector in \(W\) has the form

\[ (x,y,z,t)=(b,b-a,a,b)=a(0,-1,1,0)+b(1,1,0,1). \]

Hence one basis of \(W\) is \( ig((0,-1,1,0),(1,1,0,1) ig).\) To compute \(W^ ot\) in detail, let \(u=(x,y,z,t)\) and impose orthogonality to a basis of \(W\), namely \(v_1=(0,-1,1,0)\) and \(v_2=(1,1,0,1)\) (cf. Definition and Lemma 7.20):

\[ {\left \langle u,v_1 ight angle}=0, \qquad {\left \langle u,v_2 ight angle}=0. \]

This is the linear system

\[ -y+z=0, \qquad x+y+t=0, \]

equivalently

\[ \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 0&-1&1&0\ 1&1&0&1 \end{array} ight ) \left ( egin{array}{c} x\y\z\t \end{array} ight )=0. \]

Thus \(W^ ot\) is the kernel of this matrix. Taking \(x=a\) and \(y=b\) as free parameters gives \(z=b\) and \(t=-a-b\), so

\[ u=(a,b,b,-a-b)=a(1,0,0,-1)+b(0,1,1,-1). \]

Therefore

\[ W^ ot=L ig((1,0,0,-1),(0,1,1,-1) ig). \]

We now compute the orthogonal projection of \(u=(1,5,1,6)\) onto \(W\). We use the two basis vectors \(v_1=(0,-1,1,0)\), \(v_2=(1,1,0,1)\) of \(W\), computed in the first part. We want to apply Theorem 7.24, which requires us to have an orthogonal basis. To this end, we apply Gram–Schmidt (Proposition 7.32):

\[ {\left \langle v_2,v_1 ight angle}=-1, \qquad v_2'=v_2- rac{{\left \langle v_2,v_1 ight angle}}{{\left \langle v_1,v_1 ight angle}}v_1 =v_2+ rac12 v_1=(1, frac12, frac12,1). \]

To avoid fractions, take \(w_2:=2v_2'=(2,1,1,2)\), which is still orthogonal to \(v_1\). By Theorem 7.24, specifically the formula in Equation (7.26), for our orthogonal basis \(v_1, w_2\) we have

\[ p_W(u)= rac{{\left \langle u,v_1 ight angle}}{{\left \langle v_1,v_1 ight angle}}v_1 + rac{{\left \langle u,w_2 ight angle}}{{\left \langle w_2,w_2 ight angle}}w_2. \]

Compute \({\left \langle u,v_1 ight angle}=-4\), \({\left \langle v_1,v_1 ight angle}=2\), \({\left \langle u,w_2 ight angle}=20\), and \({\left \langle w_2,w_2 ight angle}=10\). Thus the orthogonal projection of \((1,5,1,6)\) onto \(W\) is

\[ p_W(u)=-2v_1+2w_2=(4,4,0,4). \]

Solution 7.9.3

(See Exercise 7.2.) This solution illustrates Task 7.63, Task 7.62.

The given equation describing \(U\) can be rewritten as

\[ \left ( egin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & 3 \end{array} ight ) \left ( egin{array}{c} x \ y \ z \end{array} ight ) = {\left \langle \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ -1 \ 3 \end{array} ight ), \left ( egin{array}{c} x \ y \ z \end{array} ight ) ight angle} = 0, \]

which gives, with free parameters \(y=a\) and \(z =b\), \(x = a-3b\). Thus \(U = \{(a-3b,a,b) \ | \ a,b \in { f R}\} = L((1,1,0),(-3,0,1))\).

The above equation tells us that \(U\) is the orthogonal complement of the vector \(\left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ -1 \ 3 \end{array} ight )\). Thus, writing \(L := L(\left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ -1 \ 3 \end{array} ight ))\) for the one-dimensional subspace spanned by that vector, we have \(U=L^ ot\). Therefore \(U^ ot = (L^ ot)^ ot \stackrel * L = L(\left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ -1 \ 3 \end{array} ight ))\), where the equality marked * is using Corollary 7.31.

In order to compute the projection of \(t\) onto \(U\), we apply the Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization method. The vector \(v_1 = (1,1,0)\) has norm \(|\hspace{-0.5mm}| {v_1} |\hspace{-0.5mm}| = \sqrt 2\), so that \(w_1 = rac 1 {\sqrt 2} (1,1,0)\). Then

\[ egin{align*} w'_2 := v_2 - {\left \langle v_2, w_1 ight angle} w_1 & = (-3,0,1) - \left [ (-3,0,1) \left ( egin{array}{c} 1/\sqrt 2 \ 1/\sqrt 2 \ 0 \end{array} ight ) ight ] w_1 \ & = (-3,0,1) + rac 3{\sqrt 2} rac 1 {\sqrt 2} (1,1,0) \ & = (-3,0,1) + \left( rac 32, rac 32, 0 ight) \ & = \left(- rac32, rac 32, 1 ight). \end{align*} \]

The two vectors \(v_1\) and \(w'_2\) are orthogonal, so by Theorem 7.24 (formula for an orthogonal basis) we get

\[ egin{align*} t_U & = rac{{\left \langle t, v_1 ight angle}}{{\left \langle v_1, v_1 ight angle}} v_1 + rac{{\left \langle t, w'_2 ight angle}}{{\left \langle w'_2, w'_2 ight angle}} w'_2 \ & = rac 12 (1,1,0) + rac{13/2}{11/2} \left(- rac32, rac32, 1 ight) \ & = \left( rac 12, rac 12, 0 ight) + rac{13}{11}\left(- rac 32, rac 32, 1 ight) \ & = \left(- rac{14}{11}, rac{25}{11}, rac{13}{11} ight). \end{align*} \]

An alternative way to solve this, with slightly fewer computations, is the following: since \(t= t_U + t_ ot\) is the unique decomposition, we can compute \(t_U = t - {t_ ot}\). By the positive definitness of \({\left \langle -, - ight angle}\) we have \((U^ ot)^ ot = U\) (cf. Corollary 7.31), so we can also work in \(U^ ot\) rather than in \(U\). This is somewhat simpler since \(U^ ot\) has dimension 1. Take the basis vector \(v = (1,-1,3)\) of \(U^ ot\). Then, again by Theorem 7.24 (orthogonal basis formula)

\[ egin{align*} t_ ot & = rac{{\left \langle t, v ight angle}}{{\left \langle v, v ight angle}} v \ & = rac{14}{11}(1,-1,3) \ & = \left( rac{14}{11}, - rac{14}{11}, rac{42}{11} ight). \end{align*} \]

Therefore

\[ t_U = t- t_ ot = (0,1,5) - \left( rac{14}{11}, - rac{14}{11}, rac{42}{11} ight) = \left(- rac{14}{11}, rac{25}{11}, rac{13}{11} ight). \]

Solution 7.9.4

(See Exercise 7.4.) This solution illustrates Task 7.63, Task 7.62.

\(U\) is given by the solutions of the homogeneous linear system

\[ \left ( egin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \ 1 & 1 & 1 \end{array} ight ) \left ( egin{array}{c} x \ y \ z \end{array} ight ) = \left ( egin{array}{c} 0 \ 0 \end{array} ight ). \]

The left hand matrix can be brought to reduced row echelon form using Gaussian elimination (Method 2.31): \(\left ( egin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{array} ight )\), so that \(z = a\) is a free parameter and \(x = 0\), \(y = -a\). This shows that \(U = \{ (0,-a,a) \ | \ a \in { f R}\}\) and \((0,-1,1)\) is a basis vector of \(U\). The orthogonal complement consists of vectors orthogonal to \((0,-1,1)\). As in the solution of Exercise 7.2 above, \(U\) has been defined as the orthogonal complement of the two vectors \(\left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 0 \ 0 \end{array} ight )\) and \(\left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight )\). These vectors therefore constitute a basis of \(U^ ot\).

We compute the orthogonal projection of \(t=(5,1,3)\) onto \(U\) and \(U^ ot\). This can be done using Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization as above, but also by solving the linear system

\[ \left ( egin{array}{c} 5 \ 1 \ 3 \end{array} ight ) = a \left ( egin{array}{c} 0 \ -1 \ 1 \end{array} ight ) + b \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 0 \ 0 \end{array} ight ) + c \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight ). \]

This is quickly solved as \(\left ( egin{array}{c} a \ b \ c \end{array} ight )= \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 3 \ 2 \end{array} ight )\), so that the projection of \(t\) onto \(U\) is \(a \cdot \left ( egin{array}{c} 0 \ -1 \ 1 \end{array} ight ) = \left ( egin{array}{c} 0 \ -1 \ 1 \end{array} ight )\).

Solution 7.9.5

(See Exercise 7.5.) This solution illustrates Task 7.63.

The orthogonal complement of \(T\) is given by vectors \((x,y,z)\) such that \(x-3z=0\). I.e., with free parameters \(y = a\), \(z = b\), \(x = 3b\). Thus

\[ T^ ot = L((3,0,1), (0,1,0)). \]

Solution 7.9.6

(See Exercise 7.6.) We want to find \(U\) such that \(U \oplus U^ ot = { f R}^3\) and \(t=(1,5,6) + t_ ot\), with \((1,5,6) \in U\) and \(t_ ot \in U^ ot\). This in particular means that \((1,5,6) ot t_ ot\). Solving the equation

\[ (1,1,0) = (1,5,6) + t_ ot \]

gives \(t_ ot = (1,1,0)-(1,5,6) = (0,-4,-6)\). But

\[ {\left \langle (1,5,6), (0,-4,-6) ight angle} = -20 + 36 = 16 e 0, \]

so these two vectors are not orthogonal. Therefore there is no such subspace \(U\).

We solve the second part similarly: we have \(t - (1,1,1) = (1,-1,0)\), so the Ansatz is \(t_ ot = (1,-1,0)\). We compute \({\left \langle (1,1,1), (1,-1,0) ight angle} = 0\), so these vectors are orthogonal. We now compute \(U\): \((1,1,1) \in U\), so that we can take \(U = L(1,1,1)\).

Solution 7.9.7

(See Exercise 7.7.) This solution illustrates Task 7.62.

According to Proposition 7.45, the unique point \(v \in { f R}^3\) that is lying on \(L\) and as close as possible to the origin is given by

\[ v = v_0 - p_L(v_0), \]

where \(v_0 = \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 3 \ 5 \end{array} ight )\). We will use Theorem 7.24 in order to compute this. The underlying subspace \(W\) of \(L\) is spanned by \(v_1 = \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 4 \end{array} ight )\). Renormalizing this vector to norm one, gives \(w_1 = rac{v_1}{|\hspace{-0.5mm}| {v_1} |\hspace{-0.5mm}|} = rac{v_1}{\sqrt{18}}\). This vector \(w_1\) is therefore an orthonormal basis of \(W\). We then have

\[ p_L(v_0)={\left \langle v_0, w_1 ight angle}w_1 = rac{24}{18} \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 4 \end{array} ight ) \]

and therefore

\[ v = \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 3 \ 5 \end{array} ight ) - rac{24}{18} \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 4 \end{array} ight ) = \left ( egin{array}{c} -1/3 \ 5/3 \ -1/3 \end{array} ight ). \]

This vector has norm

\[ |\hspace{-0.5mm}| {v} |\hspace{-0.5mm}| = \sqrt{27/9}= \sqrt 3. \]

Solution 7.9.8

(See Exercise 7.8.) This solution illustrates Task 7.70, Task 7.74.

The subspace \(W\) underlying \(L\) is spanned by \(\left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight )\), while the equations for \(L'\) are equivalent to \(x=z\) and \(y = z+2\). Therefore, this line has the underlying subspace \(\left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight )\) as well. By Definition 7.42, the lines are therefore parallel. Let \(w = \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight )\) be the direction vector for both lines (cf. Definition 7.50). Below, we will consider its renormalization to norm 1, which is \(w_1 = rac 1 {\sqrt3} \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight )\).

Let \(v_0 = \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 0 \ 2 \end{array} ight )\) and \(v'_0 = \left ( egin{array}{c} 0 \ -2 \ 0 \end{array} ight )\). Then the distance vector \(d = v_0 - v'_0 = \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 2 \ 2 \end{array} ight )\) is not orthogonal to \(L\) (\({\left \langle d, w ight angle} e 0\)). We compute the orthogonal projection of \(d\) onto \(W^ ot\) by computing

\[ d - {\left \langle d, w_1 ight angle} w_1 = \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 2 \ 2 \end{array} ight ) - rac 1 3 \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight ) {\left \langle d, w ight angle} = rac 13 \left ( egin{array}{c} -2 \ 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight ). \]

This vector has norm \(\sqrt{2/3}\), which is therefore the distance of \(L\) and \(L'\).

Solution 7.9.9

(See Exercise 7.9.) This solution illustrates Task 7.71, Task 7.74.

The two lines have underlying vector spaces \(W = L(\left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ -1 \end{array} ight ))\) and \(W' = L(\left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ -1 \ 2 \end{array} ight ))\) respectively. These two one-dimensional subspaces are not contained in each other: the two vectors are linearly independent.

The general point in \(L\) is of the form

\[ p(t) = \left ( egin{array}{c} 0 \ 1 \ 0 \end{array} ight ) + t \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ -1 \end{array} ight ), \]

while those on \(L'\) are of the form

\[ q(u) = \left ( egin{array}{c} 2 \ 0 \ 0 \end{array} ight ) + u \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ -1 \ 2 \end{array} ight ). \]

According to Proposition 7.45, we need to compute the values of \(t, u \in { f R}\) such that the difference \(p(t) - q(u) = \left ( egin{array}{c} t-2-u \ 1+t+u \ -t-2u \end{array} ight )\) is orthogonal on \(W\) and also on \(W'\). This leads to the linear system

\[ egin{align*} 0 & = {\left \langle \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \ -1 \end{array} ight ), \left ( egin{array}{c} -t-2-u \ 1+t+u \ -t-2u \end{array} ight ) ight angle} = t-2-u+1+t+u+t+2u=3t+2u-1, \ 0 & = {\left \langle \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ -1 \ 2 \end{array} ight ), \left ( egin{array}{c} -t-2-u \ 1+t+u \ -t-2u \end{array} ight ) ight angle} = t-2-u-1-t-u-2t-4u=-2t-6u-3. \end{align*} \]

The solution of this system is \(t= rac 67\), \(u = rac {-11}{14}\). One then computes \(p(6/7)-q(-11/14)=\left ( egin{array}{c} -5/14 \ 15/14 \ 5/7 \end{array} ight )\), whose norm is equal to \( rac5{\sqrt{14}}\). This is the distance between the two lines.

In particular, this distance is positive. This, together with the above observation that \(W e W'\) means that the lines are skew. (If one is only required to show that the lines are skew, without computing their distance, one can also solve the linear system given by the intersection of \(L\) and \(L'\); one finds out that this system has no solution, so the lines are skew.)

Solution 7.9.10

(See Exercise 7.10.) This solution illustrates Task 7.72.

A useful way to sketch lines and planes is by considering some points on them where several coordinates are zero. In the case of \(P\), three such points are \((5,0,0), (0,4,0), (0,0,2)\), while for \(L\) two such points are \((0,5,0), (0,0,5)\). This leads to the following sketch:

The equation can be rewritten as

\[ {\left \langle \left ( egin{array}{c} 4 \ 5 \ 10 \end{array} ight ), \left ( egin{array}{c} x \ y \ z \end{array} ight ) ight angle} = 20. \]

According to Definition and Lemma 7.20, the underlying vector space \(W\) is the orthogonal complement of the vector \(\left ( egin{array}{c} 4 \ 5 \ 10 \end{array} ight )\).

The line \(L\) has as its underlying vector space \(W' = L(\left ( egin{array}{c} 0 \ 1 \ -1 \end{array} ight ))\). If \(P\) and \(L\) are parallel, then \(W' \subset W\). This means that \(\left ( egin{array}{c} 0 \ 1 \ -1 \end{array} ight )\) is orthogonal to \(\left ( egin{array}{c} 4 \ 5 \ 10 \end{array} ight )\). Their scalar product is \(5-10 = -5 e 0\), so that these vectors are not orthogonal and therefore \(L\) and \(P\) are not parallel.

We compute the distance of \(P\) to the origin using Proposition 7.47:

\[ d(0,P) = rac{20}{|\hspace{-0.5mm}| {\left ( egin{array}{c} 4 \ 5 \ 10 \end{array} ight )} |\hspace{-0.5mm}|} = rac{20}{\sqrt{141}}. \]

We compute the closest point by determining the (unique) intersection point \(P \cap W^ ot\). We are thus seeking the real number \(r\) such that \(r \left ( egin{array}{c} 4 \ 5 \ 10 \end{array} ight ) = \left ( egin{array}{c} 4r \ 5r \ 10r \end{array} ight )\) lies on the plane \(P\), i.e., such that

\[ {\left \langle \left ( egin{array}{c} 4 \ 5 \ 10 \end{array} ight ), \left ( egin{array}{c} 4r \ 5r \ 10r \end{array} ight ) ight angle} = 20. \]

The left hand side equals \(r(16+25+100) = 141 r\), so that \(r = rac{20}{141}\), and therefore the point in \(P\) that is as close as possible to the origin is

\[ rac{20}{141}\left ( egin{array}{c} 4 \ 5 \ 10 \end{array} ight ). \]

Solution 7.9.11

(See Exercise 7.11.) We apply Theorem 7.38, according to which a matrix is orthogonally diagonalizable if and only if it is symmetric. This excludes \(A = \left ( egin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \ -2 & 1 \end{array} ight )\). The other two matrices are symmetric and therefore orthogonally diagonalizable. The matrix \(A = \left ( egin{array}{cc} 0 & 0 \ 0 & 0 \end{array} ight )\) is already a diagonal matrix, so for \(P = {\mathrm {id}}\) the matrix \(PAP^{-1}\) is diagonal. The standard basis vectors \(e_1, e_2\) are an orthonormal eigenbasis.

For \(A = \left ( egin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \ 2 & 1 \end{array} ight )\), we compute the eigenvalues as was indicated after Theorem 7.38.

\[ egin{align*} \lambda_{1/2} & = rac{a+d}2 \pm \sqrt{ rac{(a-d)}4 + b^2} \ & = 1 \pm 2. \end{align*} \]

Thus the eigenvalues are \(-1\) and \(3\). We compute the eigenspaces. The matrix \(A - (-1){\mathrm {id}} = \left ( egin{array}{cc} 2 & 2 \ 2 & 2 \end{array} ight )\) has kernel given by \(w_1 := e_1 - e_2\). The matrix \(A - 3 {\mathrm {id}} = \left ( egin{array}{cc} -2 & 2 \ 2 & -2 \end{array} ight )\) has kernel given by \(w_2 := e_1 + e_2\). These vectors are orthogonal:

\[ {\left \langle e_1-e_2, e_1+e_2 ight angle} = {\left \langle \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ -1 \end{array} ight ), \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight ) ight angle} = 0. \]

However, they are not normal, so an orthonormal eigenbasis for \(A\) is given by

\[ rac{w_1}{|\hspace{-0.5mm}| {w_1} |\hspace{-0.5mm}|} = rac{1}{\sqrt 2} \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ -1 \end{array} ight ) ext{ and } rac{w_2}{|\hspace{-0.5mm}| {w_2} |\hspace{-0.5mm}|} = rac{1}{\sqrt 2} \left ( egin{array}{c} 1 \ 1 \end{array} ight ). \]

(See Exercise 7.12.) If \(Av = \lambda v\), \(Aw= \lambda w\), we compute

\[ egin{align*} \lambda {\left \langle v, w ight angle} & = {\left \langle \lambda v, w ight angle} \ & = {\left \langle Av, w ight angle} \ & = (Av)^T w \ & = v^T A^T w \ & = v^T A w & ext{ since }A = A^T \ & = {\left \langle v, Aw ight angle} \ & = {\left \langle v, \mu w ight angle} \ & = \mu {\left \langle v, w ight angle}. \end{align*} \]

Solution 7.9.13

(See Exercise 7.13.) This solution illustrates Task 7.72, Task 7.74.

By definition, the hyperplane \(P\) is given by the equation

\[ {\left \langle (2,0,1,-1)^T, (x_1, \dots, x_4)^T ight angle} = 4. \]

In other words, the underlying sub-vector space \(W \subset { f R}^4\) of that hyperplane is the subspace

\[ {\left \langle (2,0,1,-1)^T, (x_1, \dots, x_4)^T ight angle} = 0, \]

or, what is the same, the orthogonal complement of \((2,0,1,-1)^T\).

The underlying subspace \(W'_t\) of the line \(L_t\) is spanned by \((t,1,0,-1)\). By Definition 7.42, \(P\) is parallel to \(L_t\) if \(W \subset W'_t\) (which is impossible for dimension reasons) or if \(W'_t \subset W\). The latter is equivalent to \((t,1,0,-1) \in W\) or, yet equivalently,

\[ {\left \langle (t,1,0,-1)^T, (2,0,1,-1)^T ight angle} = 0. \]

This equates to \(2t + 1 = 0\) or \(t = - rac12\).

We now compute the pair(s) of points realizing the minimal distance between \(P\) and \(L := L_{- rac12}\). We will use that these are exactly the points \((p, l)\) such that \(p-l ot W\) and \(p-l ot W'\).

The point \(l \in L = L_{- rac12}\) is of the form

\[ egin{align*} l & = (1,0,0,1) + r(- rac 12, 1, 0, -1) \ & = (1-s, 2s, 0, 1-2s) & ext{ with }s= rac r2. \end{align*} \]

On the other hand a point \(p =(x_1, \dots, x_4) \in P\) satisfies

\[ 2 x_1 + x_3 - x_4 = 4, \]

so we can take \(x_1 = a\), \(x_2 = b\) and \(x_3=c\) as free parameters and get \(x_4 = 2a+c-4\). That is \(p = (a,b,c,2a+c-4)\). Therefore

\[ p-l = (a+s-1,b-2s,c,2a+c+2s-5). \]

Note that \(a,b,c,s\) are the unknowns. We now determine the values of these unknowns. The orthogonal complement of \(W\), \(W^ ot = (((2,0,1,-1)^T)^ ot)^ ot = L(2,0,1,-1).\) Here we use that for a subspace \(U \subset { f R}^n\), we have

\[ (U^ ot)^ ot = U. \]

(Indeed, \(U \subset (U^ ot)^ ot\): for \(u \in U\) and \(u' \in U^ ot\) we have \({\left \langle u, u' ight angle} = 0\), so that \(u \in (U^ ot)^ ot\). Both vector subspaces of \({ f R}^n\) have the same dimension, and therefore they agree.)

This means that we are looking for \(a,b,c,s \in { f R}\) such that the following conditions are satisfied

  • \(p-l\) is a multiple of \((2,0,1,-1)\), say \(\lambda(2,0,1,-1) = (2\lambda,0,\lambda,-\lambda)\). This translates into a linear system
\[ egin{align*} a+s-1 &= 2 \lambda \ b-2s & = 0 \ c & = \lambda \ 2a+c+2s-5 & = - \lambda. \end{align*} \]
  • We now spell out the condition that \(p-l\) is orthogonal to \((- rac12, 1, 0, -1)^T\) or equivalently, to \((-1,2,0,-2)\):
\[ egin{align*} 0 &= {\left \langle p-l, (-1,2,0,-2)^T ight angle} \ & = -(a+s-1)+2(b-2s)-2(2a+c+2s-5) \ & = -5a + 2b -2c -9s + 11. \end{align*} \]

We obtain a linear system in the 5 unknowns \(a,b,c,s,\lambda\):

\[ egin{align*} a+s-2\lambda & = 1\ b-2s & = 0\ c-\lambda & = 0 \ 2a+c+2s+\lambda & = 5\ -5a + 2b -2c -9s & = - 11. \end{align*} \]

One solves this using Gaussian eliminiation (or, using an appropriate computer algebra system, cf. https://www.wolframalpha.com/input?i=Solve%28%28-5a%2B2b-2c-9s%2B11%3D0%2Ca%2Bs-1%3D2l%2Cb-2s%3D0%2Cc%3Dl%2C2a%2Bc%2B2s-5%3D-l%29%29) and obtains as solutions the vectors

\[ (a, b=4-2a, c= rac12, \lambda= rac12, s=2-a). \]

Thus

\[ egin{align*} p & = (a,4-2a, rac 12,2a- rac 72) \ l & = (1-(2-a), 2(2-a), 0, 1-2(2-a)) = (a-1, 4-2a,0,-3+2a). \end{align*} \]

This implies that for any point \(l \in L\), there is a unique point \(p \in P\) such that \((p,l)\) realizes the minimal distance between \(P\) and \(L\).

Solution 7.9.14

(See Exercise 7.14.) This solution illustrates Task 7.64, Task 7.67, Task 7.69.

For \(L\), solve the system

\[ egin{align*} x+z & = 1,\ y+z & = -2. \end{align*} \]

With parameter \(z=s\), we get \(x=1-s\), \(y=-2-s\), hence

\[ L=(1,-2,0)+L(-1,-1,1). \]

So we can take

\[ v=(1,-2,0),\qquad W=L(-1,-1,1). \]

For \(L'\), from the two given points we get

\[ L'=(0,0,1)+L(0,1,0). \]

To classify the relative position (Definition 7.42), compare direction spaces:

\[ W=L(-1,-1,1),\qquad W'=L(0,1,0). \]

These are different 1-dimensional subspaces, so \(L\) and \(L'\) are neither identical nor parallel. Now solve for an intersection:

\[ (1-s,-2-s,s)=(0,t,1). \]

From \(x=0\) we get \(s=1\), then \(z=1\) is satisfied, and \(y=t\) gives \(t=-3\). Therefore

\[ L\cap L'=\{(0,-3,1)\}, \]

so the two lines are intersecting.

Let \(P\) be the plane containing \(L\) and parallel to \(L'\). Then \(P\) is spanned by the two direction vectors

\[ d_1=(-1,-1,1),\qquad d_2=(0,1,0). \]

A normal vector can be computed by using the cross product (or by computing the orthogonal complement):

\[ d_1 imes d_2=(-1,0,-1), \]

thus \(n=(1,0,1)\) is a normal vector. Using the point \((1,-2,0)\in L\), the Cartesian equation of the plane (Proposition 7.48) is

\[ n\cdot ig((x,y,z)-(1,-2,0) ig)=0 \iff x+z=1. \]

So one valid equation of \(P\) is

\[ x+z-1=0. \]

Now fix \(l=(2,-1,-1)\in L\) and seek \(l'\in L'\) such that the line through \(l\) and \(l'\) is parallel to the plane \(x+z-1=0\). Write

\[ l'=(0,r,1),\qquad d=l'-l=(-2,r+1,2). \]

The line is parallel to the plane iff \(d\cdot (1,0,1)=0\), and indeed

\[ (-2,r+1,2)\cdot(1,0,1)=-2+2=0 \]

for every \(r\in{ f R}\). Hence every point of \(L'\) works:

\[ l'=(0,r,1),\quad r\in{ f R}. \]

For example, one can take \(l'=(0,0,1)\).

Solution 7.9.15

(See Exercise 7.15.) Let \(V=P_{\le 2}\) and \({\left \langle p,q ight angle}=\int_{-1}^1 p(t)q(t)\,dt\).

First, \({\left \langle -, - ight angle}\) is a scalar product: bilinearity and symmetry follow from properties of the integral, and for \(p eq 0\) we have \({\left \langle p,p ight angle}=\int_{-1}^1 p(t)^2dt>0\).

An orthonormal basis is the same as in the previous polynomial exercise (see Example 7.163. and Gram–Schmidt in Proposition 7.32): \(w_1= rac1{\sqrt2}\), \(w_2=\sqrt{ rac32}\,t\), \(w_3= rac{\sqrt{10}}4(3t^2-1)\).

Now consider \(f:V o V\), \(f(p)=t\,p'\). It is linear because derivative and multiplication by \(t\) are linear maps. In more detail, the conditions in Definition 4.1 are satisfied, for example \(f(p_1 + p_2) = t(p_1+p_2)' = t(p_1'+p_2') = f(p_1) + f(p_2)\) and similarly for \(f(a p)\) with \(a \in \mathbf R\). For the standard basis \(e_1=1\), \(e_2=t\), \(e_3=t^2\) we have \(f(e_1)=0\), \(f(e_2)=t=e_2\), \(f(e_3)=2t^2=2e_3\). Hence

\[ \mathrm M_{f, (e_1,e_2,e_3)}=\left ( egin{array}{ccc} 0&0&0\ 0&1&0\ 0&0&2 \end{array} ight ). \]

So \((e_1,e_2,e_3)\) is an eigenbasis, with eigenvalues \(0,1,2\).

From the above, \(\ker f=L(1)\), so \(\dim\ker f=1\); and \({\operatorname{im}\,}f=L(t,t^2)\), so \(\dim{\operatorname{im}\,}f=2\).

Finally, \(f\) has no orthonormal eigenbasis for this scalar product: the eigenspaces for \(0,1,2\) are \(L(1)\), \(L(t)\), \(L(t^2)\). These are all 1-dimensional, so any eigenbasis is (up to scaling) \((1,t,t^2)\). But \({\left \langle 1,t^2 ight angle}=\int_{-1}^1 t^2dt= rac23 eq 0\), so eigenvectors for \(\lambda=0\) and \(\lambda=2\) cannot be orthogonal.

(See Exercise 7.17.) This solution illustrates Task 7.61, Task 7.63, Task 7.62.

We compute in fact directly an orthonormal basis, which in particular is then orthogonal. (The property of also being normal is convenient further below.) We apply the Gram–Schmidt procedure (Proposition 7.32):

\[ egin{align*} w_1 & = rac 1{\sqrt 6} (1,2,0,-1), \ w_2' & = (0,-4,3,4) - {\left \langle (0,-4,3,4), rac 1 {\sqrt 6}(1,2,0,-1) ight angle} rac 1 {\sqrt{6}} (1,2,0,-1) \ & = (0,-4,3,4) + 2(1,2,0,-1) \ & = (2,0,3,2), \ w_2 & = rac 1{\sqrt {17}} (2,0,3,2). \end{align*} \]

The vectors \(w_1\) and \(w_2\) then constitute an orthonormal basis of \(U\).

We compute the orthogonal complement of \(U\) by solving the equations (for some \(v \in { f R}^4\))

\[ egin{align*} {\left \langle v, (1,2,0,-1) ight angle} & = 0 \ {\left \langle v, (0,-4,3,4 ight angle} & = 0. \end{align*} \]

We consider the matrix of the resulting linear system, and bring it into row echelon form:

\[ \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 1 & 2 & 0 & -1 \ 0 & -4 & 3 & 4 \end{array} ight ) \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 1 & 2 & 0 & -1 \ 0 & 1 & - rac 34 & 1 \end{array} ight ) \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 1 & 0 & rac 32 & 1 \ 0 & 1 & - rac 34 & 1 \end{array} ight ). \]

Thus, if \(v = (x_1, \dots, x_4)\), then \(x_3\) and \(x_4\) are free variables, so that a basis of \(U^ ot\) is given by the two vectors \((- rac 32, rac 34, 1, 0)\) and \((-1,1,0,1)\).

We compute the orthogonal projection by Theorem 7.24:

\[ egin{align*} p_U(v) & = {\left \langle v, w_1 ight angle} w_1 + {\left \langle v, w_2 ight angle} w_2 \ & = (3,2,3,1). \end{align*} \]

If we write \(w = l + l^ ot\) with \(l^ ot \in L^ ot\), then \(l^ ot = w - l = (2,-,1,0,2)-(1,1,2,0)=(1,-2,-2,2)\). This vector would need to be orthogonal to \((1,1,2,0)\), which however it is not (since their scalar product is \(-5 e 0\)). Therefore, such a subspace \(L\) does not exist.

(See Exercise 7.20.) This solution illustrates Task 7.71. The line \(M\), given to us by the systen \(x + z = 2\), \(x - 2 y = 2\) is also described as \(M = (2,0,0) + L(2, 1, -2)\). The three coordinates of the equation \(l = m\) read:

\[ egin{align*} 3+t &= 2+2s \ 1 & = s \ -t & = -2s. \end{align*} \]

The second and third equations give \(s = 1\), \(t = 2\). The first then gives \(5 = 4\), which is a contradiction. Thus, there is no point lying on \(L\) and on \(M\), i.e., \(L \cap M = \emptyset\). Thus \(L\) and \(M\) are skew, so no plane contains \(L\) and \(M\).

Solution 7.9.21

(See Exercise 7.21.) Writing \(q = (x,y,z)\) for \(x, y, z \in { f R}\), the line \(M\) is then given by \(M = p + L(q-p) = (-1,-1,-1) + L(x+1,y+1,z+1)\).

The line \(M\) will be orthogonal to \(L\) precisely if

\[ (x+1, y+1, z+1) ot (1,0,-1), \]

i.e., if \(x+1 - z - 1 = 0\), i.e., if \(x = z\).

As in the solution of Exercise 7.20 above, the line \(M\) (with \(x = z\)) intersects \(L\) precisely if the linear system

\[ egin{align*} 3+t &= -1 + s(x+1) \ 1 & = -1 + s(y+1) \ -t & = -1 + s(x+1) \ \end{align*} \]

has a solution \((s,t) \in { f R}^2\). To get a clearer view of this, we write down the augmented matrix for this linear system, keeping in mind that \(x,y\) are parameters in this linear system, and \(s, t\) are the unknowns. Note this is a linear system in two unknowns, but three equations.

\[ egin{align*} \left ( egin{array}{cc|c} -x-1 & 1 & -4 \ -y-1 & 0 & -2 \ -x-1 & -1 & -1 \end{array} ight ) & \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{cc|c} -x-1 & 1 & -4 \ -y-1 & 0 & -2 \ -2x-2 & 0 & -5 \end{array} ight ) \ & \stackrel{ ext{if } y e -1} \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{cc|c} -x-1 & 1 & -4 \ 1 & 0 & rac{2}{y+1} \ -2x-2 & 0 & -5 \end{array} ight ) \ & \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{cc|c} 0 & 1 & -4+2 rac{x+1}{y+1} \ 1 & 0 & rac{2}{y+1} \ 0 & 0 & -5+2 rac{2x+2}{y+1} \end{array} ight ). \end{align*} \]

If \(y = -1\), then we have no solution in the above system, due to the row \(0 \ 0 \ | \ {-2}\) in this case. Otherwise, for \(y e -1\), we can perform the elementary row operations as indicated above. This system has no solution if the term \(-5+2 rac{2x+2}{y+1} e 0\). If, on the contrary, the bottom right entry is zero, then the system does have a unique solution (since we then have two leading ones in the matrix). This bottom right entry is zero precisely if \(5(y+1) = 4x+4\), or, equivalently, if \(y = rac{4x -1}5\).

We therefore find that the line \(M\) through \(p\) and \(q = (x,y,z)\) intersects \(L\) orthogonally precisely if \(z = x\), \(y = rac{4x -1}5\) and \(y e -1\).

Solution 7.9.22

(See Exercise 7.22.) This solution illustrates Task 7.68.

Any line \(M\) passing through \(p\) is of the form \(M = p + L(w)\). We compute \(w = (a,b,c)\) by considering the two conditions on \(M\):

  • \(M ot L\) holds exactly if \(w ot (1,1,1)\), i.e., if \(a+b+c=0\).

  • \(M \subset P\) holds exactly if \(w ot (3,-4,1)\). Indeed, the underlying subspace of \(P\) is given by \(3x-4y+z=0\), i.e., it is the orthogonal complement of \((3,-4,1)\). That is, \(3a-4b+c=0\).

We have to solve the homogeneous linear system associated to the matrix

\[ \left ( egin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \ 3 & -4 & 1 \end{array} ight ) \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{ccc} 1 & 1 & 1 \ 0 & -7 & -2 \end{array} ight ) \]

so a solution is given by \(b = 2\), \(c = -7\) and \(a = 5\). (Any other non-zero multiple of \((5,2,-7)\) is also a solution.) Therefore

\[ M = p + L(w) = (0,1,6) + L(5,2,-7). \]

Solution 7.9.23

(See Exercise 7.23.) This solution illustrates Task 7.71, Task 7.72, Task 7.74.

The cartesian equations of the line \(L_2\) are obtained by inserting \(x = t\) into the equation for \(z\), which gives

\[ egin{align*} y & = 2 \ x+z & = 4. \end{align*} \]

We compute the intersection of \(L_1\) and \(L_2\), and at the same time determine whether they are parallel or not. The linear system for \(L_1 \cap L_2\) is (the first two equations are for \(L_2\), the latter two for \(L_1\))

\[ egin{align*} y & = 2 \ x+z & = 4\ 2x-y & = -3\ y+z & = -2. \end{align*} \]

We form the augmented matrix of this linear system and bring it into row echelon form:

\[ egin{align*} \left ( egin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 0 & 1 & 4 \ 0 & 1 & 0 & 2 \ 2 & -1 & 0 & -3 \ 0 & 1 & 1 & -2 \end{array} ight ) & \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 0 & 1 & 4 \ 0 & 1 & 0 & 2 \ 0 & -1 & -2 & -11 \ 0 & 1 & 1 & -2 \end{array} ight ) \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 0 & 1 & 4 \ 0 & 1 & 0 & 2 \ 0 & 0 & -2 & -9 \ 0 & 0 & 1 & -4 \end{array} ight ) \ & \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 0 & 1 & 4 \ 0 & 1 & 0 & 2 \ 0 & 0 & -2 & -9 \ 0 & 0 & 0 & -17 \end{array} ight ). \end{align*} \]

If we only consider the first three columns (corresponding to the three variables), the matrix has rank 3. This shows that the lines are not parallel (otherwise the rank would be 2). The full matrix has rank 4. Therefore there is no solution of the linear system and the lines are skew.

Part (2): The plane \(\pi\) contains \(L_2\) and the direction of \(L_1\) (i.e., the underlying sub-vector space) is contained in the directions of \(\pi\) (i.e., its underlying sub-vector space). We therefore first compute the direction of \(L_1\). The system

\[ egin{align*} L_1 : \ & 2x-y=-3 \ & y+z=-2 \end{align*} \]

leads to \(y = 2x+3\), \(z=-y-2=-2x-5\), in which \(x\) is a free variable. Thus

\[ egin{align} L_1 & = \{ (x, 2x+3,-2x-5) | x \in { f R} \} onumber \ & = \{(0,3,-5) + x (1,2,-2) | x \in { f R} \} \end{align} \]

(7.75)

Thus the direction of \(L_1\) is \((1,2,-2)\). (Alternatively, one may observe that the points \(p = (0,3,-5)\) and \(q = (1,5,-7)\) satisfy the linear system describing \(L_1\), so that \(L_1 = (0,3,-5) + L(q-p)\).)

The plane \(\pi\) is of the form \(\pi = \{v = (x,y,z) \in { f R}^3 | {\left \langle v, a ight angle} = d\}\) for some vector \(a = (a_1, a_2, a_3)\) and \(d \in { f R}\). I.e., \(\pi = \{(x,y,z) | a_1 x+a_2 y + a_3z = d\}\). Its underlying subvector space is \(a_1 x + a_2 +a_3z=0\). The condition \(L_1\) being parallel to \(\pi\) translates into \({\left \langle a, v_1 ight angle} = 0\), where \(v_1 = (1,2,-2)\) is the direction of \(L_1\). The condition \(L_2 \subset \pi\) translates into \({\left \langle a, v_2 ight angle} = 0\), where \(v_2 = (1,0,-1)\) is the direction vector of \(L_2\) (cf. Definition 7.50). These two conditions give the linear system

\[ egin{align*} a_1 + 2a_2 - 2a_3 & = 0\ a_1 - a_3 & = 0. \end{align*} \]

Thus \(a_1 = a_3\) and \(a_1 = 2a_2\), and \(a_2\) (say) is a free variable. We choose \(a_2 = 1\) (note: any other non-zero number \(a_2\) would eventually give rise to the same plane \(\pi\) below), so that \(a = (a_1, a_2, a_3) = (2,1,2).\) Thus, \(\pi = \{(x,y,z) | 2x+y+2z=d \}\), where we still need to compute \(d\). It is enough to choose \(d\) such that \(\pi\) contains any point of \(L_2\), for example \(e := (0,2,4)\). We compute \({\left \langle a, e ight angle} = 2 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 4 = 10\). Thus, \(d=10\), so that

\[ \pi = \{(x,y,z) | 2x+y+2z = 10 \}. \]

Part (3): By construction \(L_1\) is parallel to \(\pi\), so to compute the distance of \(\pi\) and \(L_1\), we may choose any point \(q \in L_1\) and to compute its orthogonal projection onto \(\pi\), which we call \(r\). Then \(d(\pi, L_1) = d(q, r)\).

The line passing through \(q\) and \(r\) is orthogonal to \(\pi\), so it has direction vector \((2,1,2)\). We choose \(q = (0,3,-5)\). The line passing through \(q\) and orthogonal to \(\pi\) is then of the form

\[ M = (0,3,-5) + L(2,1,2), \]

its points are of the form \(r_t = (2t,3+t,-5+2t)\) where \(t \in { f R}\). The intersection of that line with \(\pi\) is given by the unique value of \(t\) such that \(r_t \in \pi\), i.e.,

\[ 2 (2t)+(3+t)+2(-5-2t) = 10. \]

This simplifies to \(9t=17\) or \(t = rac{17}9\). Thus \(r = r_{17 / 9} = ( rac{34}9, rac{44}9, - rac{11}9)\). We then have

\[ egin{align*} d(L_1, \pi) & = d (q, r) = |\hspace{-0.5mm}| {(0,3,-5)-( rac{34}9, rac{44}9, - rac{11}9)} |\hspace{-0.5mm}| \ & = |\hspace{-0.5mm}| {(- rac{34}9,- rac{17}9,- rac{34}9)} |\hspace{-0.5mm}| = rac{17}3. \end{align*} \]

Part (4): we already know the lines are skew, so there will be unique points in \(L_1\) and \(L_2\), respectively, realizing the minimal distance. We compute these points using Theorem 7.594.. A general point \(p_t \in L_2\) is of the form \(p_t = (t, 2, 4-t)\) (with \(t \in { f R}\)). By Equation (7.75), a general point in \(L_1\) is of the form \(q_s = (s, 3+2s,-5-2s)\) (with \(s \in { f R}\)). The vector \(p_t - q_s = (s-t, 1+2s,-9-2s+t)\) needs to be orthogonal to the directions of \(L_1\) and \(L_2\), which we computed above as \((1,2,-2)\) and \((1,0,-1)\). This leads to the linear system

\[ egin{align*} 0 & = s-t+2(1+2s)-2(-9-2s+t) = 9s-3t+20 \ 0 & = s-t -(-9-2s+t) = 3s-2t+9 \end{align*} \]

This can be solved to \(t= rac 73\) and \(s = - rac{13}9\), which gives

\[ egin{align*} p_{7/3} & = ( rac 73, 2, rac 53), \ q_{-13/9}& =(- rac{13}9, - rac{17}9, - rac{19}9). \end{align*} \]

Solution 7.9.24

(See Exercise 7.24.) This solution illustrates Task 7.61, Task 7.63.

We first compute a basis of \(U\) by computing the kernel of the matrix

\[ \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \ 2 & 1 & 0 & -1 \end{array} ight ) \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \ 0 & 1 & -2 & -1 \end{array} ight ) \]

The third and fourth variables are free, and a basis of \(U\) is given by the vectors \(u_1 = (0, 1, 0, 1)\) and \(u_2 = (-1, 0, 1, 0)\). These two vectors happen to be orthogonal, so they form an orthogonal basis. (If one chooses a different basis of \(U\), one may apply the Gram–Schmidt algorithm to make them orthonormal, for example.)

Since \(\dim U^ ot = 4 - \dim U = 2\), it suffices to find any non-zero vector \(w_2 \in U^ ot\) that is also orthogonal to \(w_1\). We are thus looking for a non-zero vector in \(L((-1,0,1,0),(0,1,0,1),(1,1,-1,-1))^ ot\). This is the kernel of the matrix

\[ \left ( egin{array}{cccc} -1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \ 1 & 1 & -1 & -1 \end{array} ight ) \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 0 & 1 & 0 & -1 \ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \ 1 & 1 & -1 & -1 \end{array} ight ), \]

so if \(w_2 =(x_1, \dots, x_4)\), we obtain \(x_2 = x_4=0\) and \(x_1 = x_3\), so \(w_2 = (1,0,1,0)\) satisfies the requested properties.

The orthogonal complement \(U^\perp\) is defined by the equations

\[ egin{align*} -x_1 + x_3 & = 0 \ x_2 + x_4 & = 0. \end{align*} \]

Solution 7.9.25

(See Exercise 7.25.) This solution illustrates Task 7.70, Task 7.67, Task 7.68.

The points \(l_1 := (0,2,1)\) and \(l_2 := (2, 3, 0)\) lie in \(L\), so that the direction vector of \(L\), which is the difference of the two points, is \((2,1,-1)\).

Likewise the points \(m_1 := (1,1,2)\) and \((5,3,0)\) lie on \(M\), so that its direction vector is \((4,2,-2)\). This is a multiple of \((2,1,1)\), so the two direction vectors span the same (1-dimensional) subspace of \({ f R}^3\), so the lines are parallel.

In order to compute the plane \(P\) containing \(M\) and \(L\) we use that \(P\) is uniquely determined (since \(M e L\)) by the condition that \(l_1, l_2, m_1 \in P\). Thus

\[ egin{align*} P & = l_1 + L(l_2-l_1, m_1-l_1) \ & = (0,2,1) + L((2,1,-1), (1,-1,1)). \end{align*} \]

In order to compute the cartesian form of \(P\), we compute the orthogonal complement of \((2,1,-1)\) and \((1,-1,1)\): it is the 1-dimensional subspace of \({ f R}^3\) spanned by a vector \((a,b,c)\) satisfying \(2a+b-c=0\) and \(a-b+c=0\). This implies \(a=0\) and \(b=c\), and \(c\) is a free variable. Thus the normal vector of the plane \(P\) is \((0, 1,1)\) (or any nonzero multiple thereof). Taking into account that \((0,2,1) \in P\), we have

\[ egin{align*} P & = \{v \in { f R}^3 | {\left \langle v, (0,1,1) ight angle} = {\left \langle (0,2,1), (0,1,1) ight angle} \} \ & = \{v \in { f R}^3 | {\left \langle v, (0,1,1) ight angle} = 3 \} \ &= \{v=(x,y,z) | y+z = 3\}. \end{align*} \]

For the second part, we first compute what a general point \(q \in M\) looks like. From the above, we have

\[ M = (1,1,2) + L(4,2,-2), \]

so

\[ q = (1+4r, 1+2r, 2-2r), r \in { f R}. \]

For \(p=(0,2,1)\), this implies

\[ p-q = (-4r-1, -2r+1, 2r-1). \]

This vector needs to be orthogonal to the direction vector of \(L\), which is \((2,1,-1)\). Thus

\[ egin{align*} 0 & = 2(-4r-1)+1(-2r+1)-(2r-1) \ & = -12r \end{align*} \]

so that \(r=0\), and \(q = (1,1,2)\).

For the third part, we write \(X\) in cartesian form

\[ X= \{v \in { f R}^3 | {\left \langle v, a ight angle} = b\}. \]

We need to compute the normal vector \(a\) and the number \(b\). Since we seek \(L \subset X\), the direction vector of \(L\), \((2,1,-1)\) needs to be orthogonal to \(a = (\alpha, eta, \gamma)\), i.e.

\[ 2 \alpha + eta - \gamma = 0. \]

Moreover, the vector \(w := r-l_1=(-1,1,0)-(0,2,1) = (-1,-1,-1)\) needs to be orthogonal to \(a\) as well, since \(r\) and \(l_1 \in X\). Thus

\[ -\alpha - eta - \gamma = 0. \]

We solve this as \(\alpha = 2 \gamma\), \( eta = -3\gamma\), and \(\gamma \in { f R}\) is a free variable, which we choose to be \(1\). Thus \(a = (2, -3, 1)\). We compute \(b\) by observing that the equation defining \(X\) needs to be satisfied for \(r\), so that \(d = {\left \langle a, r ight angle} = -5\). Thus

\[ X = \{(x,y,z) | 2x-3y+z = -5 \}. \]

For the last part, we have

\[ N = r + L(w), \]

and we need to compute the direction vector \(w\) of the line \(N\). Since \(N \subset X\), we have \(w ot a = (2,-3,1)\). Since \(N ot L\), we have \(w ot (2,1,-1)\) (the direction vector of \(L\)). In other words, \(w\) is a vector in the orthogonal complement of these two vectors, or equivalently, the kernel of the matrix

\[ \left ( egin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 1 \ 2 & 1 & -1 \end{array} ight ) \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 1 \ 0 & 4 & -2 \end{array} ight ). \]

Thus for \(w = (\alpha, eta, \gamma)\), \(\gamma\) is a free variable, which we can choose to be 2, so that \( eta = rac 12 \gamma = 1\), and \(\alpha = rac{3 eta - \gamma}2= rac{3-2}2= rac 12\). Thus

\[ N = (-1,1,0) + L( rac 12, 1, 2) \]

or, equivalently, for example:

\[ N = (-1,1,0) + L(1, 2, 4). \]

Solution 7.9.26

(See Exercise 7.26.) This solution illustrates Task 7.61, Task 7.63.

We form the matrix associated to the linear system and bring it into row echelon form

\[ \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 1 & -2 & 0 & 1 \ 0 & 3 & 1 & -2 \ 3 & 0 & 2 & t \end{array} ight ) \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 1 & -2 & 0 & 1 \ 0 & 3 & 1 & -2 \ 0 & 6 & 2 & t-3 \end{array} ight ) \leadsto \left ( egin{array}{cccc} 1 & -2 & 0 & 1 \ 0 & 3 & 1 & -2 \ 0 & 0 & 0 & t+1 \end{array} ight ) \]

This matrix has rank 2 if \(t+1 = 0\), i.e., if \(t=-1\). Otherwise, for \(t e -1\), its rank is 3. We compute a basis of \(U\) for \(t=-1\) using Method 2.31. The result is \(u_1=(-2,-1,3,0)\) and \(u_2=(1,2,0,3)\).

We apply Proposition 7.32:

\[ egin{align*} w_1 & = u_1 / |\hspace{-0.5mm}| {u_1} |\hspace{-0.5mm}| = rac 1 {\sqrt{14}} u_1. \ w'_2 & = u_2 - {\left \langle u_2, w_1 ight angle} w_1 \ & = (1,2,0,3) + rac {4}{14} (-2,-1,3,0) \ & = (3/7, 12/7, 6/7, 3). \end{align*} \]

The vectors \(w_1\) and \(w'_2\) form an orthonormal basis of \(U\). (It is possible, but here not asked for, to compute \(w_2 = w'_2 / |\hspace{-0.5mm}| {w'_2} |\hspace{-0.5mm}|\), then \(w_1\) and \(w_2\) form an orthonormal basis of \(U\).)

The subspace \(U^\perp\) consists of the vectors that are orthogonal on the basis vectors of \(U\), i.e., the kernel of the matrix

\[ \left ( egin{array}{cccc} -2 & -1 & 3 & 0 \ 1 & 2 & 0 & 3 \end{array} ight ). \]

A basis of this can be computed again by Method 2.31, for example \((2,-1,1,0)\) and \((1,-2,0,1)\) form a basis of \(U^\perp\).

The subspace \(W\) will be the orthogonal complement of the line passing through \(v\) and \(w\). The direction of that line is \(v-w = (2,0,1,-3)\), so that

\[ W = \{2x_1 + x_3 -3x_4 = 0\}. \]

Solution 7.9.27

(See Exercise 7.27.) This solution illustrates Task 7.66, Task 7.68, Task 7.72.

We verify \(B \in X\) by plugging in the coordinates of \(B\):

\[ 2 \cdot 1 - 1 - 2 \cdot (-1) = 3, \]

so it satisfies the equation defining \(X\). We now compute \(C = p_X(A)\). The normal vector of the plane \(X\) is \(n = (2,-1,-2)\). Thus \(C = A - r n = (6-2r,-1+r,-4+2r)\), for some \(r \in { f R}\). In order for \(C \in X\), this needs to satisfy the equation defining \(X\), i.e.,

\[ 2(6-2r)-(-1+r)-2(-4+2r) = 3, \]

which simplifies to \(21 - 9r = 3\), i.e., \(r = 2\) and \(C = (2,1,0)\).

The plane containing the points \(A, B, C\) is of the form

\[ A + L(C-A, B-A). \]

In order to present this in cartesian form, we compute the orthogonal complement of \(C-A=(-4,2,4)\) and \(B-A=(-5,2,3)\), which is the line spanned by \(w := (-1, -4, 1)\). The plane is then given by

\[ \{x \in { f R}^3 | {\left \langle x, w ight angle} = {\left \langle A, w ight angle}\}, \]

i.e.,

\[ \{x \in { f R}^3 | -x_1 - 4x_2 + x_3 = -6\}. \]

The line that a) passes through \(B\), b) is contained in the plane \(X\) and c) is orthogonal to the line passing through \(A\) and \(B\), is given by

\[ L = B + L(v). \]

By the conditions, \({\left \langle v, B-A ight angle} = 0\) and \({\left \langle v, n ight angle} = 0\). I.e., \(v\) is any non-zero vector in the orthogonal complement of \(B-A=(-5,2,3)\) and \(n=(2,-1,-2)\), for example \(v = w = (-1,-4,1)\). Thus

\[ L =(1,1,-1) + L(-1,-4,1). \]

The direction vector of \(M_t\) can be found by choosing two points on \(M_t\), for example \(E = (0,2,-1)\) and \(F = (1,2+t,-2)\). So the direction vector of \(M_t\) is equal to \(F-E = (1,t,-1)\). This needs to be orthogonal to \(n = (2,-1,-2)\). I.e., \({\left \langle n, F-E ight angle} = 0\), which gives \(2 - t +2 = 0\), so \(t=4\).

docs.title: Linear maps
docs.text: Mathematical objects gain a lot of richness when they can be related to each other. In linear algebra, the objects of interest are vector spaces, and the way the relate to each other is by means of linear maps. The word “map” is being used as a synonym to the word “function”.

docs.title: Definition and first examples
docs.text:
Definition 4.1

Let \(V, W\) be two vector spaces. A function \(f : V o W\) is called linear (or a linear map, or a linear transformation) if it satisfies the following conditions:

\[ egin{align} f(v+v') & = f(v) + f(v') & ext{for all }v, v' \in V \end{align} \]

(4.2)

and

\[ egin{align} f(av) & = a f(v) & ext{for all }a \in { f R}, v \in V. \end{align} \]

(4.3)

The vector space \(V\) is called the domain of \(f\), \(W\) is called the codomain of \(f\).

Remark 4.4

These two conditions can be squeezed into one condition, by requiring that

\[ f(av' + a'v') = af(v) + a'f(v'), \]

for all \(a, a' \in { f R}\) and all \(v, v' \in V\). This can be paraphrased by saying that \(f\) preserves linear combinations.

Using that \(0 \cdot v = 0_V\) (the zero vector in \(V\)), the above condition implies that

\[ f(0_V) = f(0 \cdot v) = 0 \cdot f(v) = 0_W. \]

Thus, for a linear map, the zero vector of \(V\) is mapped to the zero vector in \(W\).

Example 4.5

The map \(f : { f R}^2 o { f R}^2\), \(f (x,y) := (x, -y)\) (i.e., reflection at the \(x\)-axis) is linear. This can be proven very simply algebraically: for Equation (4.2): if \(v = (x,y)\) and \(v' = (x', y') \in { f R}^2\), then

\[ f(v+v') = f((x+x', y+y')) = (x+x', -y-y') = (x,-y) + (x', -y') = f(v) + f(v'). \]

Checking Equation (4.3) is similarly simple. The linearity of the map can also be visualized geometrically:

We will soon regard the preceding example as a special case of the multiplication of a vector with a matrix, namely in this case the matrix \(\left ( egin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \ 0 & -1 \end{array} ight )\), cf. §1.2.

Example 4.6

The map

\[ D : { f R}[x] o { f R}[x], D(f) := f', \]

i.e., the derivative of \(f\), is linear. This is true because we have the formulae (proven in calculus)

\[ (f+g)'(x) = f'(x) + g'(x), (af)'(x) = a f'(x). \]

Alternatively, one may use that the derivative of a polynomial \(f(x) = \sum_{n=0}^d a_n x^n\) is given by \(f'(x) = \sum_{n=1}^d n a_n x^{n-1}\). Then, for \(g = \sum_{n=0}^d b_n x^n\), we check Equation (4.2), say:

\[ egin{align*} (f+g)'(x) & = \left (\sum_{n=0}^d (a_n + b_n) x^n ight)' \ & = \sum_{n=1}^d n (a_n+b_n) x^{n-1} \ & = \sum_{n=1}^d n a_n x^{n-1} + \sum_{n=1}^d n b_n x^{n-1} \ & = f'(x) + g'(x). \end{align*} \]

Here are a few slightly more abstract examples of linear maps, in which \(V\) is an arbitrary vector space.

Example 4.7

  • The identity map \({\mathrm {id}} := {\mathrm {id}}_V : V o V\) which is given by \({\mathrm {id}}(v) := v\) is linear.

  • For some other vector spaces \(W\), the zero map \(0 : V o W\) is the map sending every vector \(v\) to \(0_W\). It is linear.

  • For any real number \(a \in { f R}\), the map given by scalar multiplication \(V o V\), \(v \mapsto a \cdot v\) is linear. This follows from the conditions 4. and 6. in the definition of a vector space (Definition 3.10).

Non-Example 4.8

  • The map \(f : { f R} o { f R}\), \(f(x) := x^2\) is not linear. Indeed, \(f(x + y) = (x+y)^2 = x^2 + 2xy + y^2 e x^2 + y^2 =f(x) + f(y)\). Also \(f(ax) = a^2 x^2 e ax^2 = af(x)\).

  • The map \(f : { f R} o { f R}\), \(f(x) := x+1\) is not linear since again

\[ f(x+y) = x+y+1 e (x+1) + (y+1) = f(x) + f(y). \]

Thus, Equation (4.2) is violated. Also Equation (4.3) is violated: \(f(ax) = ax+1 e a(x+1) = af(x)\).

docs.title: Multiplication of a matrix with a vector
docs.text:
In this section, we define the multiplication of a matrix with a vector and show how this gives rise to a linear map. This is an extremely important way to construct linear maps.

Definition 4.9

Let \(A = (a_{ij})_{1 \le i \le m, 1 \le j \le n}\) (cf. Notation 2.22) be an \(m \times n\)-matrix and \(v = \left ( \begin{array}{c} v_1 \\ \vdots \\ v_n \end{array} \right )\) be a \(n \times 1\)-matrix, i.e., a row vector with \(n\) columns. The product of \(A\) with \(v\) is the \(m \times 1\)-vector

\[ A v := \left ( \begin{array}{c} a_{11} v_1 + a_{12} v_2 + \dots + a_{1n} v_n \\ \vdots \\ a_{m1} v_1 + a_{m2} v_2 + \dots + a_{mn} v_n \end{array} \right ). \]

Thus, the \(i\)-th entry of the (column) vector \(Av\) is computed by traversing the \(i\)-th row of \(A\) and multiplying each entry of that row with the corresponding entry of \(v\).

Example 4.10

Here are two concrete examples:

\[ \begin{align*} \left ( \begin{array}{cc} 4 & -1 \\ 2 & 1 \\ 0 & -2 \end{array} \right ) \left ( \begin{array}{c} 3 \\ 4 \end{array} \right ) & = \left ( \begin{array}{c} 4 \cdot 3 - 1 \cdot 4 \\ 2 \cdot 3 +1 \cdot 4 \\ 0 \cdot 3 - 2 \cdot 4 \end{array} \right ) = \left ( \begin{array}{c} 8 \\ 10 \\ -8 \end{array} \right ).\\ \left ( \begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \end{array} \right ) \left ( \begin{array}{c} 1 \\ 2 \\ -1 \end{array} \right ) & = \left ( \begin{array}{c} 1 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 2 + (-2) \cdot (-1) \\ {} \\ \end{array} \right ) = \left ( \begin{array}{c} 9 \\ {} \\ \end{array} \right ). \end{align*} \]

It makes perfectly good sense to consider matrices whose entries are variables. Compute:

\[ \left ( \begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \end{array} \right ) \left ( \begin{array}{c} x \\ y \\ z \end{array} \right ) = \left ( \begin{array}{c} 1 \cdot x + 3 \cdot y + (-2) \cdot z \\ {} \\ \end{array} \right ) = \left ( \begin{array}{c} x+3y-2z \\ {} \\ \end{array} \right ). \]

Thus, the equation (of column vectors consisting of 3 rows)

\[ \left ( \begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 3 & -2 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 \end{array} \right ) \left ( \begin{array}{c} x \\ y \\ z \end{array} \right ) = \left ( \begin{array}{c} 3 \\ 4 \\ -2 \end{array} \right ) \]

is a very convenient way to write down the linear system

\[ \begin{align*} x + 3y-2z & = 1 \\ y & = 4 \\ x - z & = -2. \end{align*} \]

This shows that the product of matrices with column vectors is very useful in enconding linear systems. We record this observation in the due generality:

Observation 4.11

Let

\[ A = \left ( \begin{array}{ccc} a_{11} & \dots & a_{1n} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ a_{m1} & \dots & a_{mn} \end{array} \right ) \]

be an \(m \times n\)-matrix and

\[ x = \left ( \begin{array}{c} x_1 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{array} \right ) \]

be a column vector with \(n\) rows and

\[ b = \left ( \begin{array}{c} b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_m \end{array} \right ) \]

be a column vector with \(m\) rows. Then the equation

\[ A x = b \]

is equivalent to the linear system (in the unknowns \(x_1, \dots, x_n\), consisting of \(m\) equations)

\[ \begin{align*} a_{11} x_1 + \dots + a_{1n} x_n & = b_1 \\ \vdots \\ a_{m1} x_1 + \dots + a_{mn} x_n & = b_m. \\ \end{align*} \]

docs.title: The case of \(2 \times 2\)-matrices
docs.text:
The process of multiplying a matrix with a column vector is also geometrically very important. We now investigate this in more detail in the case where

\[ A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array} \right ) \in {\mathrm {Mat}}_{2 \times 2}. \]

For a column vector \(v = \left ( \begin{array}{c} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{array} \right )\) the product is, according to Definition 4.9,

\[ Av = \left ( \begin{array}{c} a_{11} v_1 + a_{12} x_2 \\ a_{21} v_1 + a_{22} v_2 \end{array} \right ). \] \[ Av = \left ( \begin{array}{c} a_{11} v_1 + a_{12} x_2 \\ a_{21} v_1 + a_{22} v_2 \end{array} \right ). \]

(4.12)

In keeping with traditional notation from geometry, we will instead write the vector \(v\) as \(\left ( \begin{array}{c} x \\ y \end{array} \right )\), in which case

\[ Av = \left ( \begin{array}{c} a_{11} x + a_{12} y \\ a_{21} x + a_{22} y \end{array} \right ). \]

It is useful to organize this situation into a function, namely the function that sends the vector \(v\) to the vector \(Av\). We obtain a function

\[ f : {\bf R}^2 \to {\bf R}^2, v \mapsto Av \ \text{(read ``$v$ maps to $Av$''.)} \]

Of course, since \(Av\) depends on the entries of \(A\), so does this function \(f\).

docs.title: Reflections
docs.text:
Example 4.13

We consider \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{array} \right )\). According to the above we have

\[ Av = \left ( \begin{array}{c} x \\ -y \end{array} \right ). \]

We plot a few points \(v\) and the corresponding \(Av\):

Thus, geometrically, \(Av\) is the point \(v\) reflected along the \(x\)-axis.

docs.title: Rescalings
docs.text:
Example 4.14

The matrix \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} \frac 12 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array} \right )\) describes the map that compresses everything in the \(x\)-direction by the factor \(\frac 12\), and leaves the \(y\)-direction untouched.

Example 4.15

If \(r\), \(s\) are two real numbers,

\[ A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} r & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{array} \right ) \]

rescales the \(x\)-direction by a factor \(r\) (so it shrinks for \(r < 1\) and enlarges for \(r > 1\)) and rescales the \(y\)-direction by a factor \(s\).

For \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} \frac 12 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{array} \right )\), this looks as follows:

docs.title: Shearing
docs.text:
Example 4.16

For a fixed real number \(r\), the matrix

\[ A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} 1 & r \\ 0 & 1 \end{array} \right ) \]

sends \(v\) to \(Av = \left ( \begin{array}{c} x+ry \\ y \end{array} \right )\). Thus it is a shearing operation. In the following picture \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array} \right )\).

docs.title: Rotations
docs.text:
We now consider rotations.

Example 4.17

For \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array} \right )\), the vector \(Av = \left ( \begin{array}{c} -y \\ x \end{array} \right )\). Geometrically, the function \(v \mapsto Av\) is a counterclockwise rotation by \(90^\circ\).

For \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array} \right )\), the vector \(Av = \left ( \begin{array}{c} -x \\ y \end{array} \right )\) so the function \(v \mapsto Av\) describes a counterclockwise rotation by \(180^\circ\) (or, what is the same, a clockwise rotation by \(180^\circ\)).

For more general rotations, we use basic properties of the trignometric functions, e.g., as recalled in §Chapter B.

Example 4.18

In general, for any \(r \in {\bf R}\) the matrix

\[ A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} \cos r & -\sin r \\ \sin r & \cos r \end{array} \right ) \]

is such that the function

\[ v \mapsto Av = \left ( \begin{array}{c} \cos r x - \sin r y \\ \sin r x + \cos r y \end{array} \right ) \]

is a (counter-clockwise) rotation by \(r\). For this reason, \(A\) is called a rotation matrix.

In the following illustration, \(A = \left ( \begin{array}{cc} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array} \right )\).

We regard a vector \(v = \left ( \begin{array}{c} v_1 \\ \vdots \\ v_n \end{array} \right )\) as an element of \({\bf R}^n\). (Thus, instead of using the notation \((v_1, \dots, v_n)\) for an ordered tuple, as in Definition 3.1, we write the \(n\) numbers underneath in a row.) Fix an \(m \times n\)-matrix \(A\). Then the product \(Av\), which is an column vector with \(m\) entries, is an element in \({\bf R}^m\). We now regard this matrix \(A\) as fixed, and consider the vector \(v\) as a variable. In other words, we consider the function (or map)

\[ {\bf R}^n \to {\bf R}^m, v \mapsto A v. \]

Matrix multiplication has the following basic, but crucial property.

Proposition 4.19

For any \(m \times n\)-matrix \(A\), the above map is linear.

Proof. We prove this in the case \(m = n = 2\) using . (The case of general \(m\) and \(n\) is just notationally more involved, but otherwise the same.) Let \(v = \left ( \begin{array}{c} v_1 \\ v_2 \end{array} \right )\), \(v' = \left ( \begin{array}{c} v'_1 \\ v'_2 \end{array} \right )\). Then

\[ \begin{align*} A v + A v' & = \left ( \begin{array}{c} a_{11} v_1 + a_{12} v_2 \\ a_{21} v_1 + a_{22} v_2 \end{array} \right ) + \left ( \begin{array}{c} a_{11} v'_1 + a_{12} v'_2 \\ a_{21} v'_1 + a_{22} v'_2 \end{array} \right ) \\ & = \left ( \begin{array}{c} a_{11} (v_1+v'_1) + a_{12} (v_2+v'_2) \\ a_{21} (v_1+v'_1) + a_{22} (v_2+v'_2) \end{array} \right ) \\ & = A \left ( \begin{array}{c} v_1+v_2 \\ v'_1 + v'_2 \end{array} \right ) \\ & = A (v+v'). \end{align*} \]

Likewise, one checks Equation (4.3), i.e., that for \(a \in R\),

\[ \begin{align*} A (av) &= A \left ( \begin{array}{c} av_1 \\ a v_2 \end{array} \right ) \\ & = \left ( \begin{array}{c} a_{11} a v_1 + a_{12} a v_2 \\ a_{21} a v_1 + a_{22} a v_2 \end{array} \right ) \\ & =a \left ( \begin{array}{c} a_{11} v_1 + a_{12} v_2 \\ a_{21} v_1 + a_{22} v_2 \end{array} \right ) \\ & = a A v \\ & = a (A v). \end{align*} \]

◻

docs.title: Outlook: current research
docs.text:
Since matrix multiplication is such a key asset, it is of great interest to perform this process as efficiently as possible. Given two \(2 \times 2\)-matrices \(A\) and \(B\), the computation of \(AB\) by just following the definition takes 8 multiplications, namely

\[ a_{ie} b_{ej} \]

for each of the indices \(i, j, e\) being either 1 or 2. In the 1960’s an algorithm (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strassen_algorithm) was found that only requires 7 multiplications. By applying that algorithm iteratively for larger matrices, this gives a decidedly better algorithm. Current research is using methods of artificial intelligence to try and come up with similar methods for \(3 \times 3\)- and other matrices.
